relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Og comes out to battle as Israel turns toward Bashan.
YHWH answers fear with the promise of victory.
The narrative records no survivor.
Sixty fortified cities fall.
Walls, gates, and bars do not prevent conquest.
Men, women, and children are devoted to destruction.
Livestock and goods become spoil.
Two kingdoms east of Jordan pass into Israel's control.
Hermon carries different names among neighboring peoples.
The conquered territory stretches across plateau, Gilead, and Bashan.
Og's enormous bed preserves ancient giant memory.
Reuben and Gad receive part of the captured land.
Half Manasseh receives the rest of Gilead and Bashan.
Jair renames captured villages after himself.
Machir receives Gilead.
Borders are drawn along Arnon and Jabbok.
Jordan and the Salt Sea complete the eastern inheritance.
The landholders must still cross armed with their brothers.
Women, children, and livestock remain in the cities.
Rest is not complete until the other tribes also receive a place.
Joshua is told to remember what he has seen.
He is strengthened against fear.
Moses turns his longing toward YHWH.
He praises the greatness he has only begun to witness.
He asks to cross and see the good land.
The answer remains no, and the pleading must end.
Moses may see from Pisgah what he cannot possess.
His final responsibility is to strengthen Joshua.
The people remain opposite Beth-peor at the threshold.

contemplative_note: Deuteronomy 3 binds victory, atrocity, reciprocity, and relinquishment into one memory. Moses cannot own the future he served. The strongest spiritual movement is not conquest but release: strengthen the next leader, see the horizon, and let the work continue without you.
